description:
You are a security auditor who has just logged in to a host.  
    A directory that allegedly contains sensitive data already exists at  

        /home/user/audit_demo  

    and it contains exactly one file,  

        /home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt  

    Your job is to verify that permissions are too open, restrict them, and
    leave a forensic-style log.

    What you must do:

    1. Record the *current* octal permissions of  
       • the directory /home/user/audit_demo  
       • the file      /home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt  

    2. Change the permissions so that  
       • the directory is 700 (drwx------)  
       • the file is     600 (-rw-------)  

    3. Verify the change by listing the permissions again.

    4. Write a JSON log named  

           /home/user/audit_log.json  

       that captures the before-and-after state.  
       The file **must** be valid JSON, **must not** contain extra keys, and
       **must** follow this exact structure and order:

       [
         {
           "entity": "/home/user/audit_demo",
           "beforePerms": "755",
           "afterPerms":  "700"
         },
         {
           "entity": "/home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt",
           "beforePerms": "644",
           "afterPerms":  "600"
         }
       ]

       • “entity” is the full path.  
       • “beforePerms” is the three-digit numerical mode you saw **before** the
         change; “afterPerms” is what you set.  
       • Use double quotes on all keys and string values.  
       • No whitespace requirements other than being valid JSON; however, keep
         the entry order exactly as shown.

    5. Ensure the auditor (you) has read/write access to audit_log.json and that
       no other users do (i.e., mode 600).

    The automated tests will check:

    • Correct final permissions (directory 700, file 600, log 600)  
    • Exact JSON content and order as described above  
    • That the file and directory still exist and their contents were not
      modified, only their permissions.

    Hints:  
    • `stat -c "%a %n" <path>` is a concise way to read permissions.  
    • Feel free to use any standard GNU/Linux utilities.  

    Do **not** print the secret data itself; focus on permissions and the log.
truth:
<!--
        Initial state created for the learner:

        Directory: /home/user/audit_demo with mode 755
        File:      /home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt with mode 644

        File contents (must remain unchanged):

            username=admin
            password=Passw0rd!
            token=SECRETTOKEN123

        After correct completion the system must have:

        1) /home/user/audit_demo  => mode 700
        2) /home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt => mode 600
        3) /home/user/audit_log.json => mode 600, contents exactly:

        [
          {
            "entity": "/home/user/audit_demo",
            "beforePerms": "755",
            "afterPerms":  "700"
          },
          {
            "entity": "/home/user/audit_demo/credentials.txt",
            "beforePerms": "644",
            "afterPerms":  "600"
          }
        ]
    -->
name: task_000000_b64e188f
